Wow this was lovely, haven’t see a nice tribute theme like this for a while and a great grid and animation. I just wish I hadn’t read the title, or the editors had been more circumspect about it, because I read the first theme clue, popped down to the bottom and filled in WILLIAM TELL, and the rest of the theme filled itself. Or even if it had said “subject of this theme” rather than “legendary folk hero”. 
Still, can’t fault this theme or its construction, very on target all the way.
Last, I’ll get it out of the way, where does William Tell (or the Lone Ranger, as it’s usually told) take his garbage?
To da dump, to da dump, to da dump dump dump…
The 2 clues leading to the one rebus square were fortunately easy to get, of course THEBIGAPPLE and SNAPPLE.  Was a fairly fun solve.

Very fun puzzle! My only nit is that by pretty much any definition, SOLTI crossing ROSSINI is a bona fide natick. But my general rule is that one is fine — adds some suspense to when the music will play.

Very impressive construction and remarkable symmetry of the various themers. MARKSMANSHIP and SHOOTING STAR are great, as is FRUITFUL and TAKE A BOW. The embedded crossing of BOW and ARROW is excellent. 

Congratulations, Jeffrey and Evan. You really scored a bullseye.
Cute!

That was fun!

But if you’d allow me to (briefly) don my pedant hat, HTTPS is not the start of an encrypted URL.

URLs are in plaintext (as evinced by the fact that you can read them) and the protocol is there to tell you whether the _communication_ that happens between your browser and the server is encrypted (HTTPS) or unencrypted (HTTP). This is important for sites where you might want to make a purchase and as a result, provide sensitive data like your card number.

HTTPS is pretty de rigueur now, such that any site that does not support it will probably be snagged by your browser with a big red warning asking you to confirm whether you’d like to proceed.

Doffing that hat now ;-)

Rossini is well known for his opera overtures. In addition to the William Tell overture, the one for the Barber of Seville is well known. But my favorite is the overture to La Gazza Ladra, or The Thieving Magpie. It really gets going about 4 minutes 20 seconds in. Pretty sure it's been used in pop culture, i just can't recall where. https://youtu.be/PxnNUuynh_Y?si=1yNiPKwqraJcnZze Oh, another thing, before I realized there was an Apple 🍎 rebus, I thought THE BIG A was the answer. And I said to myself that is the nickname for the Aqueduct Race Track not NYC.

When William Tell ordered his arrows, he got them special dequivery.

(I'll take a bow for that one.)

Too bad Rossini's overture is in E minor (one sharp), and not C MINOR:-( Did anyone else take high school German classes in the late 1060's-70's? Did you, um, experience the teaching videos "Guten Tag" (beginner level) and "Guten Tag Wie Geht's" (intermediate-advanced), produced by the "International Film Bureau," whatever that was? Surreal yet didactic, I can still recite lines from Schiller's *Wilhelm Tell* by heart. (GTWG, episode/lesson 5): "Erlasset mir den Schuss: Hier is mein Herz!" https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FAgG6Mf1e_s "Look, listen, and you will learn German." (There is a scene in which the Hoffmann family is playing cards, using William-Tell themed cards. Apparently this is a thing: the face cards--king, queen, knave--are replaced with characters from Schiller's drama. They were especially popular in, of all places, Hungary. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/German-suited_playing_cards#Central_European) Instead of Rossini, here's a little Yodelling Song, music by William Walton and lyrics(?) by Edith Sitwell, here performed by Felicia Montealgre Bernstein. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=j6ObEkGqPL0&list=RDj6ObEkGqPL0&start_radio=1 (Some of you might recognize this scene from its re-creation--almost as garish--in the recent biopic about Leonard Bernstein.) Great puzzle, guys!

A good many of you will be old enough to remember Tom Swifties, a craze of the early ‘60s. The puzzle’s theme recalled to my mind one of my favourites: “Halt and hand over your money pouch!”, Robin Hood said, arrowgantly. For you young ‘uns, I bit of history about this so-called lowest form of humour: https://tinyurl.com/kjzktjba I believe Tom Swifties first instilled in me a love of (good) puns. (Feel free to share some of your favourite Tom Swifties.)

Rough two days.  Yesterday was a struggle and today I simply couldn't find my mistake.  I couldn't figure out why IAn wasn't the answer to "Cartesian conclusion" i.e. CartesIAN.  Of course that made no sense with the crossing nETA as the answer for "Self-referential".  But I just couldn't see it, finally gave up and looked at the answer key and am now quite annoyed with myself.

Most of the theme of the puzzle I liked.  There was a good aha moment figuring out WILLIAM TELL, then BOW crossed with ARROW.  But a bit ticked off about the single rebus square.  Yeah, it made sense, but single rebuses feel like a game time rule change.  Like the time you had to put six hyphens for a "Dashed Line" or the time there was a single number square.

Most of the puzzle was enjoyable but the last bits were frustrating.  Finding that rebus was made extra tough by some of the crossings of THE BIG APPLE and SNAPPLE being not so great.  BAGS IT when CANS IT made more sense to me.  DECIMUS a major Natick.  ORATES I'm not completely sure how that fits the close "Holds forth".

The rest of the puzzle was fun though.  For Yellow Submarine I put RINGO first and took me a while to figure out it was looking for STARR.

I pulled up the puzzle and scanned the empty grid.  First thing I saw were the gray squares – a lovely representation of a religious cross.  My eyes went to the space in the SE where it looked like the revealer went, and I saw that its letter count was just right for JESUS CHRIST.  

A most memorable cascade of imagining the possibilities followed. 

BTW, did you know that in neither the Times nor any of the other major crossword outlets has that answer ever appeared before?  Huh!

Regarding the puzzle itself, it’s one thing to come up with a puzzle idea, and a huge leap to bring it to fruition, and I’m not surprised it took Jeffrey and Evan months to pull it off.  They skillfully succeeded, IMO.  I loved the surprising rebus. 

I also believe that making a bow and arrow out of BOW and ARROW is brilliant.

Congratulations, gentlemen, on your college graduations, and thank you for a fun outing with a memorable start!

Yesterday, driving home in a car service, I was talking to the driver about his car. I know zilch about cars. He told me it was an Acura and explained how a hybrid car worked, recharging in slow traffic. He showed me the screen where that was visible.

He asked me if I drive. I told him I did a long time ago. I said I had a Saab. He knew nothing about that. I said, "You know, like a Volvo." I told him I drove across the country and back (with a friend) and that I had front wheel drive and manual transmission.

i was surprised to see the clue--2D [BygoneSwedish automaker] for SAAB in today's Midi puzzle. Synchronicity.

We talked about the price of gasoline today. I wondered if that old Saab would get better mileage than his seven-seater Acura!
Cute!

For me, 
adulting often looks like

understanding,
acknowledging what is seen,
planning ahead and 
not getting *too* caught up 
in sheer whimsy.

But when a puzzle is simply fun,
this grown-up CC 

is replaced by the other CC 
who watched balloons float away,
until they were utterly invisible.

The Dutch have a cool word,

“kinderlijk.”

It does mean “childlike," 
but more specifically it describes 
a reaction that is innocent, wide-eyed, 
and purely uninhibited,

similar to a child discovering a magical surprise.

Thank you for this Sunday whimsy 
that gave me a double dose
of kinderliik 
with the little gift 
at the very end!

Oddly enough, after I entered SEAM, Sir Georg SOLTI, and ILKS, for the "-IAM-"  I thought of WILLIAM TELL at once...a thrilling tale read in childhood.  In the version I read , he did not TAKE A BOW at all, but dropped an ARROW, declaring that, had he harmed his son, it would have been used to slay the cruel official.  (It was my Granny's book from her school days, and featured many such 'heroes,' such as Robert the Bruce.}  

I once read an entertaining piece by ROSSINI on "How to Compose an Overture," in which he describes being locked in a room by a frantic opera director; he dropped pages of the score out the window as he completed them, where a team of scribes copied them for the musicians....  He somewhat claimed that desperation enhanced his creativity and productivity.

HiYo, Silver!  Away!

Is it his birthday?

I saw the TARGET PRACTICE title, saw the "folk hero" clue, knew it would be WILLIAM TELL, didn;t care where he would eventually appear, and wrote in SWITZERLAND without a single cross.  And that's how most of the puzzle went for me.  Slam-dunky.

What were the gray squares?  They didn't look like a target and they didn't look like an apple.  Whatever.  I'd find out eventually  Oh, I see.  An ARROW pulled back in a BOW.
69A had to be THE BIG APPLE.  So why didn;t it fit?  Oh, I see -- a single rebus in the puzzle.  Single rebuses are the hardest to spot.

I was expecting OVERTURE to be one of the answers.  But ROSSINI accomplishes much the same thing, doesn't it?

So a tribute puzzle with a lot of different strings to its BOW, to use BOW in a completely different way.  But why?  Why the tribute?  So once again I ask: Is it his birthday?
One rebus?   Almost unfair…

Fun puzzle, quck Sunday ( a few seconds above my Sunday PB, thanks to flyspecking UnLIT, amd I know my birds! grr).

A nit I will pick,  however: 21A.
I don't really mind the clue (although you'll never hear the movie commando say "align watches!").
It's the 'word' itself that has bugged me for years.
The 'ch' in 'synchronize' is not really 2 letters, it's the latinization of the Greek letter Chi (X). 

Therefore the short form should be 'synch'.

I will die on this hill. Thank you for your attention to this matter.

Loved this!  Congrats on your graduation and best wishes in all that lies ahead of you both, especially many more  years of friendship.

Caitlin said it well--this puzzle hummed along nicely. It was also the rare puzzle where the theme provided the last piece. In the area around the gray squares, I had tentatively tried UNcover, MOUSSE, and was trying to fit in "towhee." While unscrambling this mess, I suddenly saw the BOW and ARROW and that area fell.

I was surprised at how much more familiar the name and sound of the overture was to me than the name of its composer.

Finally, a confession. I completely missed the rebus. When BIGAPPLE didn't fit, I figured the answer was THE BIG A and that SNA must be some shorthand for some new soft drink. The A was enough for the solve, but even with the animation, SNAPPLE didn't occur to me. Darn!
This was a breezy Sunday - not very tough, but quite entertaining. I laughed at TAKE A BOW, and the cartoon at the end was delightful. Thanks for the fun!

Fun puzzle! Felt like a perfect Sunday. It also felt surprisingly US-centric, for a theme about a European written by two Canadians! In the crossws alone: a home run derby, the ATF, SSN, UNC basketball, CNN, ABE $5 bills, and of course THE BIG APPLE and the Statue of Liberty. Whoa Nelly! Speaking of those last two, I'll repost a comment I made that's buried in a nested reply on a much earlier post: When I got the answer UnLIT, I assumed there was some hour of the night when the lights go dark on Lady Liberty, as with the Empire State Building and other UPLIT edifices. From where I live, I see the Statue of Liberty daily and at all hours — but after decades of this, it's rare that I pay close attention. So yeah, you can live in NYC and not know what some random bird is called! I did find learning about the continual evolution of the Statue's lighting to be fascinating, including this press release from 2015 about changes post Superstorm Sandy: .https://www.musco.com/news/press-statueliberty/ Thanks guys, and congratulations!! I have to say, traveling the world for a year sounds a lot more fun than going to work — but hopefully the job is just as exciting. Best of luck and safe travels!!

BUT when it is only one spot you figure you must be doing something wrong… 

Other than that, I loved the puzzle. Just enough trickery to make it tough and I loved the theme. I love it when the theme entry helps you fill out the themed entries…

The motion graphics is like gilding a lily: A lily needs nothing to enhance its perfection, thank you, and neither did this puzzle.
